aliases: Stuffed Naan with Dried Fruits and Coconut

english: Stuffed Naan with Dried Fruits and Coconut

food_subgroup: Leavened flatbread — stuffed naan with dried fruits and desiccated coconut

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002683 covers naan bread generically. No specific term exists for Peshawari stuffed naan with dried fruits and coconut; closest_parent applied.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers flat bread as a category. No specific Peshawari naan code exists; closest_parent flat bread code applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Peshawari naan or stuffed naan variants.

serving.natural_language_portion: One Peshawari naan piece typically weighs approximately 130 g, as served in Indian and Pakistani restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Naan, plain, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for naan bread (FDC ID 325871) for micronutrient detail, with adjustment for dried fruit and coconut filling based on USDA FDC entries for desiccated coconut (FDC ID 170169) and raisins (FDC ID 174683)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides plain naan values (per 100 g cooked). Peshawari naan contains a sweet filling of desiccated coconut, dried fruits, almonds, and sugar, which increases fat, sugar, and energy relative to plain naan. Values are adapted upward for fat (+4 g/100 g), sugar (+8 g/100 g), and energy accordingly, consistent with the filling composition reported in culinary literature and USDA ingredient data. This is an estimated adaptation; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. GI of naan bread (white flour, tandoor-baked) reported as 57 ± 5 (glucose reference). Peshawari naan treated as analogous to plain naan; the sweet filling (dried fruit, coconut, sugar) may modestly raise GI but no direct measurement exists.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 57 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 for plain white-flour naan (tandoor-baked). Peshawari naan is analogous in base dough; the sweet coconut-and-dried-fruit filling adds simple sugars which could raise GI slightly, but no direct measurement for Peshawari naan exists. GI 57 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. GL per serving = (57 × 50.5 × 1.30) / 100 = 37.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg/100 g; ~494 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Peshawari naan to half a piece per meal, as one full serving contributes approximately 494 mg sodium — roughly 21% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — and should request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~37 per serving) and elevated sugar content from dried fruit and coconut filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Peshawari naan as an occasional food only; its high glycemic load of approximately 37 per serving and added sugars from the sweet filling can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and portion should be restricted to no more than half a piece paired with a low-GI accompaniment.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee, butter, and desiccated coconut (~4.8 g saturated fat per 100 g; ~6.2 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease, Peshawari naan should be consumed sparingly due to its saturated fat content (~6.2 g per serving from ghee and coconut), which contributes meaningfully toward the recommended daily limit of less than 20 g saturated fat; choosing a smaller portion and avoiding additional ghee at service is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peshawari Naan is a sweet, stuffed leavened flatbread originating from Peshawar in the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa region of present-day Pakistan, and is a celebrated specialty of the broader North Indian and Pakistani tandoor-bread tradition. The dough is made from refined wheat flour (maida), yoghurt, ghee or butter, a leavening agent, and salt, then filled with a fragrant mixture of desiccated coconut, raisins or sultanas, chopped almonds, and sugar before being slapped onto the inner wall of a clay tandoor oven and baked at very high heat. The result is a soft, slightly chewy bread with a golden-brown blistered exterior, a subtly sweet and nutty interior, and a rich buttery aroma. In Singapore, Peshawari Naan is served in Indian and Pakistani restaurants as a standalone bread course or alongside curries, though its sweetness makes it more akin to a dessert bread than a savoury accompaniment. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~318 kcal/100 g) with moderate protein (~7.8 g), elevated fat from ghee and coconut (~9.2 g, of which ~4.8 g is saturated), and a high carbohydrate load (~50.5 g) with notable added sugars (~12 g) from the dried fruit filling. Its glycemic index is approximately 57 (medium) and glycemic load per serving is high (~37), making it a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome should consume only occasionally and in small portions. The saturated fat from desiccated coconut and ghee is also relevant for cardiovascular risk management. Sodium content is moderate (~380 mg/100 g) and warrants attention for those with hypertension.
common_user_queries: Is Peshawari naan good for diabetics?
Peshawari naan vs plain naan nutrition
Where to get Peshawari naan in Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Peshawari naan specifically. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain naan data with upward adjustment for the sweet coconut-and-dried-fruit filling using USDA FDC ingredient data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain white-flour naan. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base food (plain naan) is well-documented in approved sources and the filling composition is well-characterised.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates: ICMR-NIN plain naan (per 100 g cooked) forms the base; fat, sugar, and energy are adjusted upward to account for the desiccated coconut, dried fruit, almond, and sugar filling typical of Peshawari naan. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for naan, and USDA SR Legacy does not provide a reliable value for this adapted product. 3. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 for plain white-flour tandoor naan; no direct GI measurement for Peshawari naan was found in approved sources. 4. The sweet filling (dried fruit, coconut, sugar) likely raises the GI and GL above plain naan; the estimate of GI 57 may be conservative. 5. Serving size of 130 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion sizes for naan in Singapore; no standardised serving size is defined in HPB or ICMR-NIN for this specific product. 6. Allergen list includes gluten, tree nuts (almonds, coconut), and dairy; individuals with nut allergies should exercise caution. 7. The food is classified as Indian cuisine per the input record; the dish originates from the Peshawar region of present-day Pakistan and is shared across the broader South Asian culinary tradition.
